eli3: The jackknife (Quenouille 1949, Tukey 1958) estimates the variance and bias of a statistic by systematically leaving out one observation at a time. Compute the statistic on each of the n leave-one-out samples of size n−1, then combine the resulting n jackknife estimates to estimate SE and bias — without any resampling randomness.

The jackknife bias estimate: Bias_jack = (n−1)(θ̄₋ − θ̂), where θ̄₋ = (1/n)Σθ̂₋ᵢ is the mean of the leave-one-out estimates. The bias-corrected jackknife estimate: θ̂_jack = nθ̂ − (n−1)θ̄₋ = θ̂ − Bias_jack. This reduces the O(1/n) bias term in many estimators to O(1/n²).

The jackknife is deterministic (no random resampling), computationally cheaper than bootstrap (only n leave-one-out evaluations vs B), and historically important. Its main limitation: it fails for non-smooth statistics (median, quantiles, max) because removing one observation may not change the statistic at all — the jackknife variance estimate is zero or misleading.

whenWhy.title: Bias correction for smooth estimators
whenWhy.body: For estimators with O(1/n) bias (MLE of variance, correlation coefficient, many nonlinear estimators), the jackknife bias correction eliminates the leading bias term. Quenouille's original application was bias reduction for ratio estimators in survey sampling. The jackknife is exact for linear statistics and consistent for smooth nonlinear statistics.

whenWhy.title: Variance estimation for survey statistics
whenWhy.body: The jackknife is the standard variance estimation method for complex survey designs (delete-a-group jackknife, JK1, JKn). For stratified multi-stage designs: delete one PSU from each stratum in turn and compute the survey statistic on the reduced sample. The jackknife properly accounts for stratification and clustering in variance estimation — used in NHANES, NAEP, and most national surveys.

whenWhy.title: Acceleration estimation for BCa bootstrap CI
whenWhy.body: The acceleration constant â in the BCa bootstrap CI is estimated via the jackknife: â = (1/6)Σᵢ(θ̄₋ − θ̂₋ᵢ)³ / [Σᵢ(θ̄₋ − θ̂₋ᵢ)²]^{3/2}. The jackknife provides a computationally efficient way to estimate the rate at which the SE of θ̂ changes with the true parameter value — a key component of the BCa correction.

deepDiveIntro: The jackknife computes the statistic n times, each time leaving out one observation. The variance estimate uses the spread among those n leave-one-out values: multiply the sum of squared deviations from their mean by the factor (n minus 1) divided by n. Equivalently, each leave-one-out estimate can be turned into a pseudovalue, and the variance of those pseudovalues gives the same result.

For smooth estimators, each leave-one-out estimate is approximately the full-sample estimate minus the influence of that observation divided by n. This connects the jackknife to influence-function theory: the jackknife variance estimate approximates the sum of squared influence values divided by the sample size, which is also the nonparametric sandwich variance estimator.

deepDive.heading: Jackknife Variance Estimator
deepDive.body: The jackknife estimates variability by recomputing the statistic after leaving out one observation at a time. If those leave-one-out versions vary a lot, the original estimator is unstable; if they vary little, it is stable. For linear statistics such as the sample mean, the jackknife reproduces the usual variance formula exactly.

deepDive.heading: Bias Reduction via Jackknife
deepDive.body: The jackknife can also reduce bias by combining the full-sample estimate with the average of the leave-one-out estimates. For many smooth estimators, this removes the leading small-sample bias term. That is why the jackknife is historically important not just as a variance estimator but also as a bias-correction tool.

deepDive.heading: Jackknife vs Bootstrap
deepDive.body: The jackknife is deterministic and computationally cheap, while the bootstrap is more flexible and generally more accurate for complicated statistics. For smooth estimators the two often agree closely, but the bootstrap handles non-smooth statistics much better. In practice, the jackknife is often chosen for speed or for survey designs, and the bootstrap for broader applicability.

deepDive.heading: Delete-d Jackknife
deepDive.body: A more general version of the jackknife removes several observations at once instead of just one. This helps when the ordinary leave-one-out version is too fragile or fails for non-smooth statistics such as quantiles. It sits conceptually between the classical jackknife and the bootstrap.
deepDive.formula: delete-d: remove d observations per replicate

takeaways: Jackknife: leave out one observation at a time; Var_jack = [(n−1)/n]Σ(θ̂₋ᵢ − θ̄₋)². [1]
Bias correction: θ̂_jack = nθ̂ − (n−1)θ̄₋; removes O(1/n) bias term. [1]
Deterministic and requires only n evaluations; cheaper than bootstrap. [1]
Fails for non-smooth statistics (median, quantiles, max) — use bootstrap instead. [1]
Key use case: survey variance estimation (delete-a-group jackknife) and BCa acceleration constant. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does the jackknife fail for the sample median?
edgeCases.a: The sample median θ̂ = x_(⌈n/2⌉) is not a smooth functional of the empirical distribution. Removing one observation typically doesn't change the median value at all (for odd n, the median is unchanged unless the removed observation was the median itself). So θ̂₋ᵢ ≈ θ̂ for most i, giving Var_jack ≈ 0. This is incorrect — the true sampling variance of the median is π/(2n) · (1/f(θ))² where f is the density at the median. The bootstrap correctly estimates this via the distribution of bootstrap medians. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the relationship between the jackknife and the influence function?
edgeCases.a: The influence function IF(x;T,F) = lim_{ε→0} (T(F_ε) − T(F))/ε measures the effect of an infinitesimal contamination at point x on the estimator T, where F_ε = (1−ε)F + εδ_x. The empirical influence value for observation i is approximately: (n−1)(θ̂₋ᵢ − θ̂) ≈ −IF(xᵢ;T,F̂). So the jackknife provides finite-sample approximations to influence values, connecting it to robust statistics and the sandwich variance estimator. [1]

sources.label: Efron & Hastie — Computer Age Statistical Inference, Ch. 11
Davison & Hinkley — Bootstrap Methods and Their Application, Ch. 2
